coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of Ti3+/N/B-TiO2@NGQDs composite photocatalyst with significantly enhanced visible light catalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Li
Chen, Feng
Tang, Xinde
Zhang, Zhenwen
Liu, Shuilin
Liu, Ning
Zhang, Chunyan
coredata.dc:description: A two-step hydrothermal process was used to successfully create Ti3+, N, and B co-doped TiO2 modified with nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (Ti3+/N/B-TiO2@NGQDs (TNBTN)) composite photocatalyst for photodegradation of bisphenol A (BPA) and methyl orange (MO) under visible-light illumination. The prepared TNBTN composite demonstrated significantly enhanced visible light catalytic performance. Especially, the apparent rate constant of BPA degradation with TNBTN was approximately 56.9 times that of pure TiO2, which was attributed to broader visible light absorbance and faster transmission and separation of photoinduced charge. In addition, the mechanism of BPA and MO degradation with TNBTN was distinct.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co-doped
TiO2
NGQDs
Wastewater treatment
Visible light

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the N and B co-doped TiO2 /NGQDs (NBTN) composite photocatalyst
Preparation of the TNBTN composite photocatalyst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hunan Provincial College Students' Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of China
Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of TiO2, NBT, NBTN, TNBT, and TNBTN.
TEM image (a) and HRTEM image (b) of TNBTN.
XPS spectra of TNBTN: (a) Ti 2p and (b) O1s.
UV–visible diffuse reflectance absorption spectra (a) and calculated band gap (b) of TiO2, NBT, NBTN, TNBT, and TNBTN.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of BPA and (b) the corresponding pseudo-first-order plot over the as-synthesized photocatalysts;(c) Photocatalytic degradation of MO and (d) the corresponding pseudo-first-order plot over the as-synthesized photocatalysts.
Effect of scavengers on the degradation of BPA (a) or MO (b) over the TNBTN photocatalyst under visible light illumination.
Proposed visible-light photocatalytic mechanism of TNBTN composite towards BPA or MO photodegradation.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of Ti3+/N/B-TiO2@NGQDs composite photocatalyst with significantly enhanced visible light catalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Hunan Institute of Technology, Hengyang 421002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Hunan Institute of Technology, Hengyang 421002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Economics and Management, Hengyang Normal University, Hengyang 421002, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Economics and Management, Hengyang Normal University, Hengyang 421002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity of the TNBTN composites could be ascribed to the synergistic effects of broader visible light absorption and faster transport and separation of photoinduced e−/h+ pairs.

A two-step hydrothermal process was used to successfully create Ti3+, N, and B co-doped TiO2 modified with nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (Ti3+/N/B-TiO2@NGQDs (TNBTN)) composite photocatalyst for photodegradation of bisphenol A (BPA) and methyl orange (MO) under visible-light illumination. The prepared TNBTN composite demonstrated significantly enhanced visible light catalytic performance. Especially, the apparent rate constant of BPA degradation with TNBTN was approximately 56.9 times that of pure TiO2, which was attributed to broader visible light absorbance and faster transmission and separation of photoinduced charge. In addition, the mechanism of BPA and MO degradation with TNBTN was distinct.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Faced with increasingly serious environmental problems, differentways have been adopted to improve the environment, among which semiconductor photocatalysis technology is a very promising pollution control technology [1–4]. At present, the photocatalyst with excellent performance and the most in-depth research is titanium dioxide semiconductor, which is widely used in wastewater treatment [5,6], solar cells [7,8], photocatalyticwatersplitting [9–11], and lithium-ion batteries [12] due to its characteristics [13,14]. However, as a photocatalyst material, TiO2 nanomaterials still face two major challenges in practical applications: (i) TiO2 cannot absorb visible light in the sunlight due to its semiconductor characteristics. (ii) The quantum efficiency of TiO2 as a photocatalyst material is still low due to its high recombination and low migration rates of photogenerated charge. Therefore, great efforts were committed to promoting the visible light performance of TiO2 via widening its light absorbance and accelerating the transmission and separation of photoinduced charge.

On the one hand, ion doping for TiO2 has been confirmed to be a valid strategy for expanding its light absorption. In the nonmetallic ion doping of TiO2, nonmetallic elements co-doping can be good for improving its visible light absorption performance [15–17]. Abdelraheem et al. developed N and B codoped TiO2 (NBT) to achieve a redshift of light absorption [18]. However, the visible light absorption of NBT still has significant room for improvement. In the metal ion doping of TiO2, Ti3+ self-doping has many advantages: (i) it will not introduce other impure substances, so it is an ideal doping method to effectively maintain the appearances and structures of TiO2. (ii) Below the conduction band of TiO2, it forms an intermediate level and oxygen vacancy (Ov), which narrows its band gap [19]. Thus, its light absorption extends to the visible light region. In addition, as an ideal electron trap, Ov can prevent the recombination of photoinduced carriers [14]. Therefore, we speculated that two non-metallic elements (such as N, B) and Ti3+ co-doping for TiO2 should be able to further expand its visible light absorption performance, and also enhance its visible light degradation activity.

On the other hand, the recombination of TiO2 with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can promote the transmission and separation of the photoinduced charge of TiO2. As a zero-dimensional graphene material, GQDs exhibit rich photochemical and photophysical performance such as photogenerated electron transfer and redox characteristics, and fluorescence due to their special edge effect and surface structure [20,21]. N-doped GQDs (NGQDs) can promote the active regions on the surface of GQDs to directly participate in the oxygen reduction reactions [22,23]. Therefore, NGQDs are ideal electronic mediators and carriers, which can inhibit the recombination of photoinduced charges [24]. The currently developed series of TiO2/ NGQDs have superior photodegradation performance than pristine TiO2 [25–28]. However, the visible light photocatalytic effect of these composite photocatalysts cannot meet the practical application. Thus, such composite photocatalysts need to be further developed to improve visible light absorption.

In this work, the TNBTN composite photocatalyst was first prepared via a simple two-step hydrothermal technique, and its visible light photoactivity was investigated by degrading BPA and MO solution. Moreover, the stability of the TNBTN composite photocatalyst during the cyclic degradation of the BPA solution was tested, and the trapping tests were carried out to ascertain the oxidized substances during the photocatalytic degradation. Finally, a possible photodegradation mechanism of MO and BPA over the TNBTN composite photocatalyst was also suggested. We expect that this study will provideguides for the design and preparation of visible-light-drivenTiO2-basedphotocatalystswith highefficiencyand stability.
The NGQDs were prepared by thermal decomposition of C6H5O7(NH4)3 based on Yin’s research, with a few modifications (see supplementary material) [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the N and B co-doped TiO2 /NGQDs (NBTN) composite photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The NBTN composite photocatalyst was hydrothermally synthesized. Firstly, under continuous stirring, 1 mL of acetic acid, 3.5 mL of tetrabutyl titanate, and the solution that dissolved 0.1614 g of borane particles were successively added dropwise to a 50 mL beaker containing 25 mL absolute ethanol. After that, 0.06 g of NGQDs and 1.0 mL of ultrapure H2O were added to the previous mixed solutions under continuous agitating for 60 min. Finally, the mixture was diverted to a 50 mL hydrothermal reactionvessel, put into a muffle furnace, and reacted at 180 °C for 20 h. The resulting brown coffee precipitates were gathered by centrifuging, and dried at 90 °C for 8 h. As a comparison, pristine TiO2 and NBT were also synthesized in the same way without adding borane particles and NGQDs or NGQDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the TNBTN composite photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The TNBTN composite photocatalyst was hydrothermally synthesized. Typically, 1.0 g NBTN and 0.25 g Zn were added to 20 mL ethanol and ground for 30 min. After that, the mixed solutions were migrated to a hydrothermal reactor and held in a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 24 h. The collected powder after cooling was washed with hydrochloric acid under magnetic stirring to remove excess Zn, and then the washed mixture was centrifuged and cleaned with ultrapure water until forming a neutral solution. Finally, the obtained pellet was dried in a vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h. As a comparison, Ti3+, N, and B co-doped TiO2 (TNBT) was synthesized via the same method with NBT as a precursor.

An X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical Xpert Pro, Netherlands) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) was used to identify the crystal structure of the products. A transmittance electron microscope (Talos F200S, USA) was utilized to investigate the morphology and nanocrystalline nature of the samples. An ESCALAB Xi + spectrometer with Al Ka X-ray sources was utilized to investigate the surface chemical composition of the samples. A UV-2700 spectrophotometer equipped with a diffuse reflection device was used to study the light absorption properties of the products.

A CHI750E electrochemical workstation with a typical three-electrode cell was employed to implement the photoelectrochemical tests. The electrodes were prepared (see supplementary material). Photocurrent tests were performed in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution under visible light irradiation. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy tests were implemented in 2.5 mM K3Fe(CN)6 solution, and Mott-Schottky tests were conducted in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution at 1000 Hz.

The visible light photodegradation performance of the photocatalysts toward BPA degradation was initially investigated at room temperature in a quartz reactor. In detail, the photocatalysts (100 mg) were scattered into the 100 mL of BPA (10 mg/L) solution to form the suspension, which was kept agitating for 60 min in the dark to reach a sorption–desorption balance before radiation. Then, the suspension solution was illuminated by a 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter while being stirred violently. The mixed solution (3 mL) was withdrawn at intervals of 10 min, centrifuged, and filtered by a 45 μm water system membrane. The concentration of residual BPA was determined by an HPLC (Shimadzu LC-15, Japan). Methyl orange (MO, 10 ppm) was also used to study the photodegradation activities of the photocatalysts. The concentration of residual MO was measured at 464 nm by a UV-2700 UV–vis spectrophotometer.

To ascertain the oxidized substances during the photocatalytic degradation, the trapping tests were carried out, and the test resembled the previous photocatalytic activity measurement. 1 mM of isopropanol (IPA), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), and ammonium oxalate (AO) were separately selected as the capture agents for •OH, •O2 −, and h+, and dosed into the BPA or MO solution before light reaction.

Fig. 1 describes the XRD patterns of the as-synthesized photocatalysts, which revealed matching XRD patterns. The distinctive diffraction peaks of five photocatalysts belonged to the anatase phase (JCPDS card No. 21-1272), and no impure peaks else were observed, which means the structure of catalysts does not change after N and B co-doping, introducing NGQDs, and Zn reduction. In particular, no distinctive peaks of NGQDs were found due to their low amounts and significant scattering in the NBTN and TNBTN photocatalyst [27]. The TNBT and TNBTN photocatalysts displayed an anatase (101) peak with slightly elevated intensity compared with the pristine TiO2, NBT, and NBTN, which should be caused by the higher reaction temperature and the longer reaction time in their preparation process.

The sizes and shapes of five photocatalysts were detected by TEM. Fig. S1a, b, c, d, and Fig. 2 a showed that five photocatalysts were uniform nanoparticles of irregular shape. Their particle sizes were not much different, and about 10–20 nm by counting about 100 particles (Fig. 2a). The TEM images of NGQDs were shown in Fig. S1e and f, and the sizes of NGQDs were observed to be about 2–5 nm. Besides, the microstructures of the TNBTN composites were resolved through HRTEM. Fig. 2b demonstrated the existence of both TiO2 and NGQDs nanoparticles in the TNBTN composite. A clear lattice spacing of 3.5 Å completely matched the crystal faces (101) of the anatase phase [18,30]. Meanwhile, the interplanar spacing of 2.1 Å matched the crystal face (100) of GQDs [23].

XPS analysis of the TNBTN composite was carried out to study its chemical compositions and bonding configuration. The survey spectrum of the TNBTN composite in Fig. S2a indicated that it consisted of Ti, C, O, N, and B. In addition, the scarcity of zinc proved that zinc had been completely removed. Fig. 3 a shows the Ti 2p spectrum of the TNBTN composite is resolved into five peaks. Four strong peaks allocated at 464.4, 464.0, 458.7, and 458.3 eV belonged to the Ti4+ and Ti3+ state in TiO2 for Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 levels, respectively [11], indicating that some Ti4+ in TiO2 should be smoothly converted to Ti3+ by Zn under hydrothermal conditions. The weak peak allocated at 460.5 eV was corresponding to the Ti-N and Ti-B bonds [18]. Fig. 3b depicts the O1s spectrum of the TNBTN composite, which could be resolved into four peaks. The strong peak at 529.9 eV belonged to the Ti4+–O–Ti4+bond, and the three weak peaks at 530.2, 531.4, and 532.2 eV came from the Ov adjacent to Ti3+, O-Ti-N bond, and surface hydroxyl (OH) groups of TiO2, respectively [11,18,31,32]. Therefore, it indicated that Ti3+ and Ov existed in the TNBTN composite. Fig. S2b displays the C1s high-resolution scan of the TNBTN composite, which was decomposed into three distinctive peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.6 eV corresponding to the CC, CO, and O-CO bonds, respectively [27,33]. The resolved fitting spectrum of B 1s (Fig. S2c) showed that three peaks at the binding energies of 189.3, 191.5, and 192.6 eV were attributed to the Ti-B bond, Ti-O-B bond, and H3BO3 or B2O3 on the surface of the TNBTN composites, respectively [34]. As for the N1s peak (Fig. S2d), it was resolved into two peaks at 400.0 and 400.5 eV corresponding to the Ti-N bonding of TNBT and pyrrolic N of NGQDs, respectively [15,35]. The XPS results verified that Ti3+, N, and B were doped into TiO2, and coexisted with NGQDs in the TNBTN composite.

The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of the five photocatalysts were represented in Fig. 4 a. Due to the N and B co-doping, and the formation of the intermediate level (Ti3+ 3d and Ov) [31], the absorption edge of NBT and TNBT showed a sequential red-shift compared to pristine TiO2. Moreover, the combination of NBT or TNBT with NGQDs resulted in further red-shift in the spectra, which may lead to more photogenerated charges [36]. The band gap (EG) of TiO2, NBT, and TNBT are 2.98, 2.88, and 2.74 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b). The value of TiO2 was narrower compared to the reportorial anatase TiO2 (about 3.2 eV) [37], which may be attributed to carbon atoms doping [18].

The catalytic performance of the obtained photocatalysts was initially investigated via degrading BPA under visible light radiation. Fig. 5 a represents the adsorption/photodegradation of BPA over five photocatalysts. BPA was not obviously adsorbed by the TiO2, NBT, and TNBT photocatalysts after 60 min of dark equilibration, and slightly adsorbed by the NBTN and TNBTN composite photocatalysts. BPA was not also significantly degraded without any photocatalyst present (blank) due to its poor light absorbance at λ > 300 nm [38]. Compared with pristine TiO2, the visible-light photodegradation performance of other photocatalysts was all improved. Especially, the TNBTN composite photocatalyst notably showed the best photodegradation ability, and the photodegradation rate of BPA over it reached 94.6 % within 30 min of visible light illumination. The visible light catalytic performance of five photocatalysts was further studied according to the photolysis degradation kinetics. Fig. 5b displays the change of BPA concentration with illumination time, which was in accord with the pseudo-first-order kinetic curve. The apparent rate constant (k) for pure TiO2, NBT, TNBT, NBTN, and TNBTN are 0.00237, 0.01320, 0.01807, 0.02213, and 0.13487 min−1, respectively. Similarly, the TNBTN composite photocatalyst also exhibited the maximum k value, which was successively 56.9, 10.2, 7.5, and 6.1 times that of pure TiO2, NBT, TNBT, and NBTN, indicating that the photocatalytic performance of TNBTN under visible light was significantly enhanced by N, B, and Ti3+ co-doped and introducing NGQDs.

To further testify to the performance of the TNBTN photocatalyst, colored MO degradation was conducted under visible light. Similarly, the TNBTN photocatalyst displayed the best photodegradation activity and the Maximum value of k (Fig. 5 c and d). Within 30 min of visible light illumination, the photodegradation rate of MO over the TNBTN photocatalyst reached 96.0 %, and the value of k for it was 0.10786 min−1.

Reusability is one of the most important indicators to measure the efficiency of a catalyst. Thus, five photocatalytic degradation cycles of BPA over the TNBTN photocatalyst were carried out. The TNBTN photocatalyst displayed excellent reusability, and its photodegradation activity hardly decreased after repeated use five times (Fig. S3a), which confirmed that the TNBTN photocatalyst was stable. Before and after 5 cycles of degradation, no detectable changes in the XRD patterns (Fig. S3b) of the TNBTN photocatalyst further indicated its stability.

The charge transfer and separation efficiency of five photocatalysts were determined by the photocurrent response and EIS. Fig. S4a displays the photocurrent intensity of the TiO2, NBT, TNBT, NBTN, and TNBTN photocatalysts. The TNBTN photocatalyst displayed the greatest photocurrent response, and the TNBTN and NBTN photocatalysts exhibited a greater photocurrent response than the TNBT and NBT photocatalysts, respectively, meaning the more efficient charge separation efficiency owing to the photogenerated electrons can be rapidly transferred by NGQDs. Fig. S4b displays the EIS Nyquist plots of the five photocatalysts. Similarly, the TNBTN photocatalyst exhibited the smallest arc radius among the five catalysts, reflecting the highest charge-transferring efficiency. The TNBTN and NBTN photocatalysts displayed a smaller arc radius than the TNBT and NBT photocatalysts, respectively, which meant that NGQDS recombination improved charge transfer efficiency. Therefore, it could be reasonably deduced that the TNBTN photocatalysts could show the highest photodegradation activity, and introducing NGQDs could enhance the photodegradation performance of the catalysts.

To verify the major reactive species responsible for BPA and MO degradation by the TNBTN photocatalyst, trapping experiments were performed. Fig. 6 a exhibits the results of the trapping tests of BPA degradation, implying that three scavengers reduced the photocatalytic activity of the TNBTN photocatalyst to different extents, which suggested that •O2 −, h+, and •OH were all responsible for BPA degradation. However, adding BQ only slightly reduced BPA degradation, while adding AO or IPA significantly decreased BPA degradation, which revealed that h+ and •OH were the major oxidizing substances in the degradation process of BPA over the TNBTN photocatalyst. However, for MO degradation (Fig. 6b), the case was changed. IPA addition cannot block the photodegradation of MO, AO addition obviously decreased MO degradation, and BQ addition entirely prevented MO degradation. It was implied that •O2 − and h+ were the major active substances during MO photodegradation over the TNBTN photocatalyst. The above results showed that the photodegradation mechanism of BPA and MO by the TNBTN was different.

To further explain the photodegradation mechanism of the TNBTN composite, it is vital to ensure the locations of the conduction-band (CB) potential (ECB) and valence-band (VB) potential (EVB) of TiO2 and TNBT. At the same time, the ECB in numerous n-type semiconductors is 0.2 V lower than the flat potential (Vfb) [39–41], which is generally measured by Mott-Schottky [42]. Fig. S5 shows the Mott–Schottky plot of TiO2 and TNBT. The slope of the line was positive, which indicated that both TiO2 and TNBT were n-type semiconductors. Their Vfb were respectively determined to be approximately −0.62 and −0.48 V vs Ag/AgCl at pH 7 (equal to −0.39 and −0.25 V vs NHE at pH 7) [43,44], respectively. Thus, their ECB were approximately −0.59 and −0.45 V, respectively. The EG of the TiO2 and TNBT were respectively 2.98 and 2.74 eV (see Fig. 4b), and thus their EVB were 2.39 and 2.29 V, respectively.

Scheme 1 illustrated a possible photodegradation mechanism of BPA and MO over the TNBTN photocatalyst based on the above observations and discussions. First, N and B co-doping of TiO2 caused the formation of an intermediate level above its VB, such as N 2p and B 2p [17]. In addition, Ti3+ and Ov can generate a sub-level below its CB. The two mid-levels synergistically reduced its band gap and expanded its optical absorption. What is more, the NGQDs could also widen their optical absorption. Therefore, TNBT could be effectively excited to produce electron (e−) and hole (h+). Second, the NGQDs and Ov were good electron sinks and reservoirs, which were conducive to the rapid transferring and separation of photoinduced charges [24]. Therefore, the enhanced photodegradation performance of the TNBTN photocatalyst was ascribed to the synergism of broader visible light absorption and faster transport and separation of photoinduced e-/h+ pairs.

In photocatalysis, the excited e− migrated from the VB of TNBT to the mid-level composed of N 2p and B 2p, then to the intermediate level composed of Ti3+ and Ov, and finally to the CB of TNBT. e− could rapidly migrate into NGQDs, and be trapped by the dissolved oxygen to generate potent oxidized •O2 − radicals since the CB potential (-0.59 eV) of TNBT was more negative than O2/ •O2 − potential (-0.33 eV) [45]. Due to the ability of the weak acidic BPA solution to provide H+, most •O2 – reacts with H+ to convert into •OH by electron reduction (equations (1) and (2)). However, Weak alkaline methyl orange solution makes it difficult to produce H+. As for the photogenerated h+ remained on the VB of TNBT, its potential (2.39 eV) was lower than that OH−/•OH potential (2.40 eV) and H2O/•OH potential (2.72 eV) [24], and thus it could not react with OH− or H2O to produce •OH. Finally, these generated •O2 −, •OH, and h+ could oxidize BPA, leading to its decomposition, while generated •O2 − and h+ could oxidize MO, which resulted in its decomposition.
(1) •O2 − + e− + 2H+ → H2O2
(2) H2O2 + e− → •OH + OH−

A hydrothermal technique was adopted to synthesize TNBTN hybrid material, which displayed a significantly enhanced visible light photodegradation activity for BPA or MO compared with pure TiO2, NBT, TNBT, and NBTN. The enhanced photodegradation activity should be due to the cooperative effects of the broader visible light absorbance and faster transmission and separation of charge carriers. For the TNBTN photocatalyst, h+ and •OH mainly contributed to BPA photodegradation, while •O2 − and h+ were chiefly responsible for MO photodegradation. Furthermore, the TNBTN photocatalyst displayed very high stability. Such TiO2-based photocatalysts will possess practical uses for the treatment of wastewater.

Li Dai: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Feng Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xinde Tang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhenwen Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Shuilin Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Ning Liu: Conceptualization. Chunyan Zhang: Methodology, Investigation.
